From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [ISU Junior Grand Prix](/w/index.php?title=ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix&redirect=no "ISU Junior Grand Prix"))

Recurring figure skating series

For the current season, see [2025–26 ISU Junior Grand Prix](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix "2025–26 ISU Junior Grand Prix").

| ISU Junior Grand Prix of Figure Skating | |
| --- | --- |
| [Logo of the ISU Junior Grand Prix](/wiki/File:ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix_logo.png) | |
| Status | Active |
| Genre | [ISU Junior Grand Prix](/wiki/ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix "ISU Junior Grand Prix") |

The **ISU Junior Grand Prix of Figure Skating** – originally called the **ISU Junior Series** – was established by the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU) in 1997 and consists of a series of seven international [figure skating competitions](/wiki/Figure_skating_competition "Figure skating competition") exclusively for junior-level skaters. The locations of the JGP events change every year. Medals may be awarded in [men's singles, women's singles](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"), [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating"), and [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance"). While all seven competitions feature the men's, women's, and ice dance events, only four competitions each season feature the pairs event. Skaters earn points based on their results each season, and the top skaters or teams in each discipline are then invited to compete at the [Junior Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final](/wiki/ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "ISU Junior Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final").[[1]](#cite_note-:02-1) Beginning with the [2008–09 figure skating season](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_figure_skating_season "2008–09 figure skating season"), Junior Grand Prix Final has been held in conjunction with the [Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final](/wiki/Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Skaters are eligible to compete on the junior-level circuit if they are at least 13 years old before July 1 of the respective season, but not yet 19 (for [single skaters)](/wiki/Single_skating "Single skating"), 21 (for men and women in [ice dance](/wiki/Ice_dance "Ice dance") and women in [pair skating](/wiki/Pair_skating "Pair skating")), or 23 (for men in pair skating).[[3]](#cite_note-3) Competitors are chosen by their respective skating federations. The number of entries allotted to each ISU member nation in each discipline is determined by their results at the prior [World Junior Figure Skating Championships](/wiki/World_Junior_Figure_Skating_Championships "World Junior Figure Skating Championships"). Each skater or team is limited to participating in no more than two Junior Grand Prix competitions per season.[[4]](#cite_note-2025Schedule-4)

Switzerland hosted the very first Junior Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final, the culminating event of the Junior Grand Prix series, in 1997 in [Lausanne](/wiki/Lausanne "Lausanne"). [Timothy Goebel](/wiki/Timothy_Goebel "Timothy Goebel") of the United States won the men's event,[[5]](#cite_note-JGP1997Final-5) and also became the first skater in the world to successfully perform a [quadruple Salchow](/wiki/Salchow_jump "Salchow jump") in competition,[[6]](#cite_note-6) and the first American skater to land a quadruple jump of any kind in competition.[[7]](#cite_note-7) At the [2002 Junior Grand Prix Final](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix "2002–03 ISU Junior Grand Prix") in [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague"), [Miki Ando](/wiki/Miki_Ando "Miki Ando") of Japan became the first woman to land a [quadruple jump](/wiki/Quadruple_jump "Quadruple jump") in competition when she performed a quadruple Salchow.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Reaction to the COVID-19 pandemic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Reaction to the COVID-19 pandemic")]

On April 30, 2020, the [International Skating Union](/wiki/International_Skating_Union "International Skating Union") (ISU) established a working group, chaired by ISU Vice-president for Figure Skating Alexander Lakernik, to monitor the ongoing [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"). Its responsibilities included determining the feasibility of holding events as scheduled, possibly behind closed doors, during the first half of the [2020–21 season](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_figure_skating_season "2020–21 figure skating season"); and the financial impact of any potential cancellations. The ISU also announced that any host federation needed to make a decision regarding cancellation of their Junior Grand Prix (JGP) event at least ten weeks prior to the event.[[9]](#cite_note-ISU-23202-9)

On May 16, the Slovak Figure Skating Association informed the ISU that it had cancelled all events that it was scheduled to host due to the pandemic, including the second event of the [2020–21 Junior Grand Prix series](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix "2020–21 ISU Junior Grand Prix").[[10]](#cite_note-SVK-Cancelled-10) On May 26, [Skate Canada](/wiki/Skate_Canada "Skate Canada") cancelled the first event of the JGP series.[[11]](#cite_note-CAN-Cancelled-11) On July 3, the [Japan Skating Federation](/wiki/Japan_Skating_Federation "Japan Skating Federation") cancelled its event in [Yokohama](/wiki/Yokohama "Yokohama"), originally scheduled to be the fourth in the series.[[12]](#cite_note-JPN-Cancelled-12) The Japan Skating Federation later announced on July 13 that it would not send any skaters to any Junior Grand Prix event, even if the competitions proceeded as scheduled.[[13]](#cite_note-JSF-071320-13)

On July 13, the ISU announced major changes to the JGP format, including:

* No ISU Junior Grand Prix points would be awarded and no ISU Junior Grand Prix Ranking for 2020–21 would be established.[[14]](#cite_note-ISU-071320-14)
* There would be no pre-allocated entries for ISU members to participate in each Junior Grand Prix event and ISU members could choose the events in which they would enter their skaters.[[14]](#cite_note-ISU-071320-14)

At this point, there were only five JGP events still on the schedule: Hungary, the Czech Republic, Uzbekistan, Slovenia, and Latvia.[[14]](#cite_note-ISU-071320-14) On July 20, the ISU officially cancelled all events of the series, citing increased travel and entry requirements between countries and potentially excessive sanitary and health care costs for host members.[[15]](#cite_note-2020Cancelled-15)

Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-18)** Armenia was originally scheduled to host the fourth Junior Grand Prix competition in [Yerevan](/wiki/Yerevan "Yerevan"), but it was cancelled following the [Azerbaijani invasion of Armenia](/wiki/September_2022_Armenia%E2%80%93Azerbaijan_clashes "September 2022 Armenia–Azerbaijan clashes").[[16]](#cite_note-16) The event was not moved, and skaters scheduled to attend were reallocated to the events in either Poland or Italy.[[17]](#cite_note-17)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-19)** Canada hosted a Junior Grand Prix event in [Richmond](/wiki/Richmond,_British_Columbia "Richmond, British Columbia") in addition to the [Junior Grand Prix Final](/wiki/ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "ISU Junior Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final") in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver").
3. **[^](#cite_ref-20)** Canada was originally scheduled to host the first Junior Grand Prix event in [Richmond](/wiki/Richmond,_British_Columbia "Richmond, British Columbia"), but [Skate Canada](/wiki/Skate_Canada "Skate Canada") cancelled it due to the COVID-19 pandemic.[[11]](#cite_note-CAN-Cancelled-11)
4. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Canada/France2021_22-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Canada/France2021_22-1) Canada was originally scheduled to host the second Junior Grand Prix event in [Edmonton](/wiki/Edmonton,_Alberta "Edmonton, Alberta"), but [Skate Canada](/wiki/Skate_Canada "Skate Canada") cancelled the event due to uncertainties surrounding the COVID-19 pandemic. It was instead allotted to France, who hosted two competitions back-to-back in [Courchevel](/wiki/Courchevel "Courchevel").[[18]](#cite_note-21)
5. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-2020Cancelled_23-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-2020Cancelled_23-1) [***c***](#cite_ref-2020Cancelled_23-2) [***d***](#cite_ref-2020Cancelled_23-3) [***e***](#cite_ref-2020Cancelled_23-4) The ISU officially cancelled all of the Junior Grand Prix events for the [2020–21 season](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix "2020–21 ISU Junior Grand Prix") due to the COVID-19 pandemic.[[15]](#cite_note-2020Cancelled-15)
6. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-France/China2024_24-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-France/China2024_24-1) France was originally scheduled to host the seventh Junior Grand Prix competition in [Épinal](/wiki/%C3%89pinal "Épinal"), but withdrew the event. It was instead allotted to China, who hosted the competition in [Wuxi](/wiki/Wuxi "Wuxi").[[21]](#cite_note-ISU2634-29)
7. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Croatia/Poland2022_27-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Croatia/Poland2022_27-1) Croatia was originally set to host the fifth Junior Grand Prix event in [Zagreb](/wiki/Zagreb "Zagreb"), but the Croatian Skating Federation cancelled it, citing "logistical reasons".[[19]](#cite_note-ISU240222-25) It was instead allotted to Poland, who hosted two back-to-back competitions in [Gdańsk](/wiki/Gda%C5%84sk "Gdańsk").[[20]](#cite_note-26)
8. **[^](#cite_ref-28)** France hosted a Junior Grand Prix event in [Saint-Gervais-les-Bains](/wiki/Saint-Gervais-les-Bains "Saint-Gervais-les-Bains") in addition to the [Junior Grand Prix Final](/wiki/ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "ISU Junior Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final") in [Marseille](/wiki/Marseille "Marseille").
9. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-Italy_30-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-Italy_30-1) Italy hosted a Junior Grand Prix event in [Egna](/wiki/Neumarkt,_South_Tyrol "Neumarkt, South Tyrol") in addition to the [Junior Grand Prix Final](/wiki/ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "ISU Junior Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final") in [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin").
10. **[^](#cite_ref-31)** Japan was originally scheduled to host the fourth Junior Grand Prix event in [Yokohama](/wiki/Yokohama "Yokohama"), but the [Japan Skating Federation](/wiki/Japan_Skating_Federation "Japan Skating Federation") cancelled the event due to the COVID-19 pandemic.[[12]](#cite_note-JPN-Cancelled-12)
11. **[^](#cite_ref-33)** The [2021 Junior Grand Prix Final](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "2021–22 Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final") was cancelled due to the situation with the [Omicron variant](/wiki/SARS-CoV-2_Omicron_variant "SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variant") in Japan.[[22]](#cite_note-2021Cancellation-32)
12. **[^](#cite_ref-35)** After the cancellation of the Junior Grand Prix events scheduled in Canada and Slovakia, an event in [Riga](/wiki/Riga "Riga") was added to the calendar.[[23]](#cite_note-34) Ulimately, the ISU officially cancelled all of the Junior Grand Prix events for the [2020–21 season](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix "2020–21 ISU Junior Grand Prix") due to the COVID-19 pandemic.[[15]](#cite_note-2020Cancelled-15)
13. **[^](#cite_ref-36)** Slovakia was originally scheduled to host the second Junior Grand Prix event in [Košice](/wiki/Ko%C5%A1ice "Košice"), but the Slovak Figure Skating Association cancelled the event due to the COVID-19 pandemic.[[10]](#cite_note-SVK-Cancelled-10)
14. **[^](#cite_ref-37)** Spain hosted a Junior Grand Prix event in [Logroño](/wiki/Logro%C3%B1o "Logroño") in addition to the [Junior Grand Prix Final](/wiki/ISU_Junior_Grand_Prix_of_Figure_Skating_Final "ISU Junior Grand Prix of Figure Skating Final") in [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona").
15. **[^](#cite_ref-39)** The Junior Grand Prix competition in [Scottsdale, Arizona](/wiki/Scottsdale,_Arizona "Scottsdale, Arizona"), was cancelled following the [September 11 terrorist attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks").[[24]](#cite_note-38)
